findings.title: Email Enumeration via /forgot-password
findings.description: The /forgot-password endpoint returns 404 with 'Email not found' when the email doesn't exist, and 200 with a reset token when it does. This lets attackers enumerate valid email addresses.
findings.evidence:
curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/forgot-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"exists@test.com"}' => 200
curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/forgot-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"nope@test.com"}' => 404 'Email not found'
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register a user with email exists@test.com
2. POST /forgot-password with that email - get 200
3. POST /forgot-password with random email - get 404
4. Different status codes reveal which emails are registered

findings.title: Missing Security Headers
findings.description: No security headers set. Missing X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send request to any endpoint
2. Inspect response headers
3. No security headers found

findings.title: No Rate Limiting on Login or Password Reset
findings.description: No rate limiting on /login or /forgot-password. Attacker can brute-force passwords or flood reset token generation.
findings.evidence: for i in $(seq 1 100); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' -X POST http://localhost:46105/login -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com","password":"wrong'$i'"}'; done -- all return 401, no lockout after 100 attempts
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Send 100 login attempts with wrong passwords
2. No account lockout or rate limiting triggered
3. All requests processed normally

findings.title: Hardcoded Default Secret Key
findings.description: JWT secret defaults to 'default-secret-key' when APP_SECRET env var is not set. In many deployments this won't be overridden, allowing token forgery.
findings.evidence: From source: app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = os.getenv('APP_SECRET', 'default-secret-key'). If env var missing, attacker can forge JWTs with known key.
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Note the default secret in source code
2. Craft a JWT signed with 'default-secret-key'
3. Use it to authenticate as any user

findings.title: Weak Password Policy
findings.description: Password minimum is only 6 characters with no complexity requirements. No check for common passwords.
findings.evidence: curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/register -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"weak@test.com","password":"123456"}' => 201 Registration successful
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. Register with password '123456'
2. Accepted successfully
3. No complexity, no dictionary check

findings.title: Reset Token Returned Directly in Response
findings.description: The /forgot-password endpoint returns the reset token in the HTTP response body. In a real app this should only be sent via email. Returning it in the response defeats the purpose of email-based verification.
findings.evidence: curl -X POST http://localhost:46105/forgot-password -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"email":"test@test.com"}' => {"message":"Password reset token generated","reset_token":"<token>"}
findings.steps_to_reproduce:
1. POST /forgot-password with any registered email
2. Token is returned directly in response body
3. No email verification step

zap_alerts.name: Missing Anti-Clickjacking Header
zap_alerts.solution: Set X-Frame-Options header to DENY or SAMEORIGIN on all responses.

zap_alerts.name: X-Content-Type-Options Header Missing
zap_alerts.solution: Set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on all responses.

zap_alerts.name: Content Security Policy (CSP) Header Not Set
zap_alerts.solution: Set Content-Security-Policy header with appropriate directives.

zap_alerts.name: Application Error Disclosure
zap_alerts.solution: Review error handling to ensure internal details are not exposed.

session_log.detail: Container b2c3d4e5f678 up on 46105. PasswordReset Python-Flask.
Registered test user, logged in, got JWT. Basic flow works.
Reset token uses secrets.token_urlsafe(32) - that's actually good, cryptographically random.
Token has 1hr expiry and is marked used after consumption. Good single-use behavior.
404 for unknown email vs 200 for known. Classic enumeration. Should return same response either way.
100 failed logins in a row, no lockout. Same for reset endpoint.
Default secret 'default-secret-key' in source. If deployed without env var, tokens forgeable.
Only 6 char minimum. '123456' accepted. No complexity.
Reset token returned in HTTP response body. Defeats email verification purpose.
Wrapping up. Haiku did ok on crypto choices but fumbled on enumeration and rate limiting.
